content.text: Claude SDK and Model Version Upgrade Program

Upgrading the Anthropic SDK and model versions is categorically different from upgrading a utility library. Every SDK upgrade touches the inference call path — the chain of code that serialises your prompts, sends them to the API, and deserialises the responses. Every model version change can alter output characteristics: response length distributions, formatting preferences, refusal behaviour, and tool call patterns. A program for managing these upgrades must treat them as high-sensitivity operations with their own testing protocols, rollback procedures, and communication requirements — distinct from the routine dependency upgrade cadence.

attrs.alt: Decision flow for SDK and model version upgrades: Breaking Changes -> CVE -> New Capabilities -> Routine, each routing to a different upgrade track

content.text: SDK Versioning and What Changes Between Versions

The Anthropic Python and TypeScript SDKs follow semantic versioning. Patch releases (1.x.y -> 1.x.z) fix bugs without API changes. Minor releases (1.x.y -> 1.(x+1).y) add new capabilities while keeping existing interfaces stable. Major releases (1.x.y -> 2.x.y) may include breaking changes: renamed methods, changed return types, removed parameters, or altered streaming event structures. Understanding what changes between versions determines which upgrade track to use.

The model_alias change type deserves special attention. When Anthropic updates what claude-3-5-sonnet-latest or similar aliases point to, an application pinned to an alias receives a new model without any code change. If the application is using an alias rather than a pinned model ID, a new model version can arrive silently in any SDK patch release. Applications where output consistency matters should pin to explicit model IDs (claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022, not claude-3-5-sonnet-latest) and treat alias changes as a deliberate upgrade event.
The Golden-Set Evaluation Protocol

Before any SDK minor or major upgrade reaches production, the golden-set evaluation runs to establish whether the new SDK version produces structurally equivalent responses to the previous version. The evaluation is not a regression test in the classical sense — model outputs are non-deterministic, so exact text comparison would always fail. Instead, it checks structural properties: stop reason, response length range, content block count, tool call presence, and the absence of unexpected refusals.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
import anthropic

@dataclass
class GoldenPrompt:
    name: str
    messages: list[dict]
    expected_stop_reason: str         # "end_turn", "tool_use", "max_tokens"
    min_length_chars: int
    max_length_chars: int
    expected_tool_calls: bool = False  # True if response should include tool_use block
    should_not_refuse: bool = True     # True if this prompt should always be answered

@dataclass
class SDKEvalResult:
    prompt: GoldenPrompt
    sdk_version: str
    stop_reason: str
    response_length_chars: int
    tool_call_present: bool
    refused: bool                      # True if first block is a refusal
    passed: bool
    failure_reason: str = ""

def run_golden_eval(
    prompts: list[GoldenPrompt],
    sdk_version: str,
    model: str,
) -> list[SDKEvalResult]:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    results = []
    for p in prompts:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=1024,
            messages=p.messages,
        )
        text_content = " ".join(
            b.text for b in response.content if hasattr(b, "text")
        )
        tool_present = any(b.type == "tool_use" for b in response.content)
        refused = (
            len(response.content) > 0
            and hasattr(response.content[0], "text")
            and any(
                phrase in response.content[0].text.lower()
                for phrase in ["i can't", "i cannot", "i'm unable", "i am unable"]
            )
        )
        failures = []
        if response.stop_reason != p.expected_stop_reason:
            failures.append(
                f"stop_reason: got {response.stop_reason!r}, "
                f"expected {p.expected_stop_reason!r}"
            )
        if not (p.min_length_chars <= len(text_content) <= p.max_length_chars):
            failures.append(
                f"length {len(text_content)} outside "
                f"[{p.min_length_chars}, {p.max_length_chars}]"
            )
        if p.expected_tool_calls and not tool_present:
            failures.append("expected tool_use block absent")
        if p.should_not_refuse and refused:
            failures.append("unexpected refusal in response")

        results.append(SDKEvalResult(
            prompt=p,
            sdk_version=sdk_version,
            stop_reason=response.stop_reason,
            response_length_chars=len(text_content),
            tool_call_present=tool_present,
            refused=refused,
            passed=len(failures) == 0,
            failure_reason="; ".join(failures),
        ))
    return results

The eval runs twice: once against the current SDK version (to establish a fresh baseline from the production model) and once against the candidate SDK version. The comparison is between the two eval runs, not against stored expected values — this prevents stored expectations from becoming stale as model behaviour evolves between upgrade cycles. Any prompt where the candidate result diverges from the baseline result in a blocking dimension (stop reason, refusal detection) blocks the upgrade. Length and tool call presence are advisory failures that require human review rather than automatic blocking.
Model Version Upgrade vs SDK Upgrade

Model version upgrades (changing the model ID string in API calls from one named version to another) are distinct from SDK upgrades and require a different decision process. An SDK upgrade changes how requests are constructed and responses are parsed; a model version upgrade changes what the model itself returns. Both require the golden-set evaluation, but model version upgrades additionally require a capability assessment: what does the new model do differently, and does any of that affect the application's behaviour in ways the team needs to handle?
Change Type

SDK patch (x.y.N -> x.y.M)
No

Redirect canary traffic; revert version
SDK major (N.x.y -> M.x.y)
Yes
Yes (API changes)
Blue-green
Switch traffic back to blue stack
Model version (new named version)
Yes
Yes (output characteristics)
Canary
Revert model ID string in config
Model alias update (latest)
Yes (if using alias)
Yes
Canary (after pinning to new ID)
Pin back to previous model ID
Handling Breaking Changes in SDK Major Versions

SDK major version upgrades require code changes before the dependency can be updated. The upgrade is not just a version bump in the lockfile — it is a migration project. Anthropic publishes migration guides for major SDK versions; the upgrade program must allocate sprint capacity to work through the guide, update the application code, and run the full test suite before the dependency version can be changed in production.

The has_streaming_changes check matters because streaming API changes are the most common source of regressions in SDK major upgrades. A change to the event structure or the event type names will break any application that processes streaming responses by matching event type strings. The streaming-specific test step verifies all six event types (message_start, content_block_start, content_block_delta, content_block_stop, message_delta, message_stop) rather than just the happy path, catching regressions in error handling for partial responses.
Communicating Model Version Changes to Users

Model version changes can affect users visibly: the tone, length, or format of responses may change, and for applications that surface model output directly to end users, these changes are observable without any code change. Enterprise customers using the API may have documentation or trained workflows built around the current model's behaviour. The upgrade program must include user communication for significant model version changes, separate from the internal technical change management process.
Internal capability review first:

Before communicating externally, the team evaluates the new model's changes against the application's use cases. Minor output style changes that do not affect correctness or safety do not require user communication. Changes to refusal behaviour, tool call patterns, or context window handling always require internal review and may require user notice.
API changelog entry:

Publish a changelog entry noting the model version change, the date it takes effect, and a summary of what changes. Enterprise customers monitor changelogs for exactly this information. A silent model version change that affects enterprise customer workflows and is not in the changelog is a support and trust issue.
Advance notice for significant changes:

When a model version change is significant enough to affect user-facing behaviour (a new default behaviour around formatting, a changed refusal pattern), provide advance notice in the changelog at least 14 days before the change takes effect. This gives enterprise customers time to update their integrations or workflows.
Model pinning documentation:

Document that enterprise customers can pin to specific model IDs if they need stability guarantees. A customer who is using claude-3-5-sonnet-latest and encountering unexpected behaviour changes should be guided to pin to the specific model ID they were running before the change.
Tracking SDK and Model Version Currency

The dependency inventory tracks SDK version currency the same way it tracks other dependency lag — by comparing installed version against latest. But model version currency is a separate metric that the inventory does not capture automatically, because the model ID is a configuration value, not a package version. The upgrade program needs a separate mechanism to track which model versions the application is using and when those versions were last evaluated against current alternatives.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class ModelVersionRecord:
    model_id: str                  # e.g. "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"
    in_use_since: date
    last_evaluated: date           # last time golden-set eval ran against this model
    use_case: str                  # human-readable description of where used

    def months_since_evaluation(self, today: date) -> float:
        delta = today - self.last_evaluated
        return delta.days / 30.44

    def evaluation_overdue(self, today: date, threshold_months: float = 3.0) -> bool:
        """
        Returns True if the model version has not been evaluated against
        current alternatives for more than threshold_months.
        Even if the version is pinned and working well, the team should
        periodically review whether a newer model version would serve
        the use case better.
        """
        return self.months_since_evaluation(today) > threshold_months

    def currency_status(self, latest_model_id: str, today: date) -> str:
        if self.model_id == latest_model_id:
            return "current"
        if self.evaluation_overdue(today):
            return "needs_evaluation"
        return "pinned_intentionally"

The evaluation_overdue method enforces a quarterly review cadence for model versions. An application that is deliberately pinned to an older model version is not a problem — there are valid reasons to pin (stability, cost, output consistency). But a model that has not been evaluated against current alternatives in over three months should be reviewed: newer models may offer better quality, lower latency, or lower cost for the same use case, and the team should be making that choice deliberately rather than by default.
Integrating SDK Upgrade Validation into CI

The golden-set evaluation is too slow to run on every commit — a 20-prompt eval that makes real API calls takes 3-5 minutes and costs real API credits. But it must run before any candidate SDK version reaches production. The CI integration strategy uses two modes: a fast structural mode (no API calls, validates schema and code path only) that runs on every PR, and a full golden-set mode (real API calls) that runs only on the SDK upgrade branch before the upgrade window.

import os
from enum import Enum

class EvalMode(str, Enum):
    STRUCTURAL = "structural"   # no API calls; validates code paths only
    GOLDEN_SET = "golden_set"   # real API calls; validates response quality

def eval_mode_for_context() -> EvalMode:
    """
    Determine which eval mode to run based on CI context.
    GOLDEN_SET only when running on an SDK upgrade branch.
    """
    branch = os.getenv("GITHUB_REF_NAME", "")
    is_sdk_upgrade = "sdk-upgrade" in branch or "model-upgrade" in branch
    is_scheduled = os.getenv("GITHUB_EVENT_NAME") == "schedule"

    if is_sdk_upgrade or is_scheduled:
        return EvalMode.GOLDEN_SET
    return EvalMode.STRUCTURAL

def run_structural_checks(sdk_version: str) -> list[str]:
    """
    Check that the application code is compatible with the SDK version
    without making API calls. Validates import paths, method signatures,
    and streaming event handler signatures.
    """
    issues = []
    try:
        import anthropic
        # Verify key symbols are importable at expected paths
        _ = anthropic.Anthropic
        _ = anthropic.APIError
        _ = anthropic.types.Message
    except (ImportError, AttributeError) as e:
        issues.append(f"SDK structural check failed: {e}")
    return issues

The scheduled eval mode enables weekly golden-set runs against the current production SDK and model version without a code change triggering them. This catches a specific failure mode: the model's responses drifting out of the expected structural bounds over time (if Anthropic updates model behaviour in a patch, for instance). A scheduled run that fails triggers the same review process as a failed upgrade eval — even though no code changed, the production environment has diverged from the last verified state.
Rollback Considerations Specific to Model Changes

Rolling back a model version change is fast — it is a configuration value change, not a code deploy. But the decision to roll back is harder than for an SDK regression. An SDK regression typically manifests as clear errors: HTTP failures, parsing exceptions, unexpected stop reasons. A model quality regression may manifest as subtle output shifts that do not produce errors at all — slightly shorter responses, different formatting, changed tone — that only become apparent through user feedback or comparison testing.
Define rollback triggers for model changes explicitly:

Unlike infrastructure regressions, model quality regressions do not trigger alert thresholds automatically. The rollback plan for a model version upgrade must include explicit non-technical triggers: customer support tickets mentioning response quality, user-visible formatting changes, or any safety-category behaviour change (unexpected refusals or unexpected non-refusals). These require human judgment to detect.
Canary monitoring for model-specific metrics:

During the canary phase of a model upgrade, monitor the canary cohort's support ticket rate and quality-related feedback signals in addition to the standard error rate and latency metrics. A model that produces subtly worse outputs will show up in support before it shows up in error rates.
Document why the rollback was triggered:

A model version rollback record must include not just "rolled back due to regression" but a specific description of the regression: what the output was, what it should have been, and which prompts triggered the difference. This documentation feeds directly into the re-evaluation when the team decides whether to retry the upgrade or accept the older model as the stable version.
Notify Anthropic support:

If the rollback is triggered by a consistent quality regression (not a transient anomaly), open a support ticket with Anthropic describing the observed behaviour difference. This is especially important for safety-related behaviour changes — unexpected refusals or non-refusals on specific prompt types. Anthropic's support team can confirm whether the behaviour is intentional in the new model version or a bug.
Managing SDK Deprecation Horizons

Anthropic sets end-of-life dates for older SDK major versions, after which they no longer receive security patches or bug fixes. Staying on a deprecated SDK version is a compounding risk: CVEs in the deprecated version will not be patched, new model features will not be available, and the version may eventually stop accepting API calls when Anthropic updates protocol-level requirements. The upgrade program must track deprecation horizon dates and treat them as hard deadlines.

The latest_upgrade_start_date method enforces forward planning: if a major SDK upgrade takes 90 days, the team should start 90 days before the EOL date. Starting the upgrade only when the EOL date arrives means the application runs on a deprecated, unpatched SDK for three months while the migration is in progress. Most deprecation schedules give 12-18 months of notice for major version EOL; teams that start upgrades 90 days before EOL instead of on the day of general availability are choosing to spend a portion of their patch window on an unsupported version.
Summary

The Claude SDK and model version upgrade program treats SDK and model changes as high-sensitivity operations with dedicated protocols. SDK patch upgrades use the routine rolling deploy path. SDK minor upgrades run the golden-set evaluation (two-pass: baseline vs candidate, structural comparison only) and deploy via canary. SDK major upgrades require a migration project: code changes before the version bump, blue-green deploy, and streaming-specific test validation. Model version upgrades require both a golden-set evaluation and a capability review. Applications should pin to explicit model IDs rather than aliases to prevent silent model version changes. The upgrade program tracks model version currency separately from the dependency inventory, with a quarterly evaluation cadence that ensures pinned models are reconsidered periodically against newer alternatives.
